what_it_does: Creates the public.connector_audit table — the append-only audit log for the remote MCP gateway. Each row records one remote tool call: id (the call_id uuid), ts, subject (authed principal), scope, tool, args_preview (truncated), outcome (OK/DENY/TEACHING-ERROR/DISPATCH-ERROR), and detail. Adds three indexes (ts desc, tool, outcome), a table comment, and grants ALL to service_role/postgres plus SELECT to anon/authenticated.
observations: id is text primary key holding the call_id (a uuid stored as text)
outcome is a free-text column with an enumerated set documented in a comment, not a CHECK/enum constraint
RLS is intentionally left OFF — the header comment explains it is a system audit log gated by JWT role, not row policies
Both anon and authenticated get SELECT (the operator UI reads via these roles); the gateway writes via service_role which bypasses RLS
Header comment states audit-write failure causes the call to fail loud (mandatory-audit guard, attributed to a 'lead ruling')
All DDL is idempotent (create table/index if not exists)
standouts: The mandatory-audit-or-fail-loud invariant declared in the comment (write must succeed or the tool call fails)
Deliberate RLS-OFF decision with documented rationale (append-only system log)
conventions: Heavy explanatory header comment + table comment (self-description discipline)
Grant pattern: service_role/postgres ALL, read roles SELECT
concerns: outcome and scope are free text with values only enforced by comment, not by a DB constraint — drift possible
anon SELECT exposes the full audit log to the public anon key (intentional per comment, but worth noting)
notes: The mandatory append-only audit table for the remote MCP gateway; RLS off by design, write-or-fail-loud, read by operator UI.
questions: Is the args_preview truncation length enforced anywhere, or only by the writer?
Why anon SELECT rather than restricting reads to authenticated only?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — stated purpose (mandatory audit table for the remote MCP gateway) is exactly what the DDL implements.
apparent_themes: mandatory auditing / accountability for remote calls
outbound-only architecture (prod table lives in Tim's Supabase, gateway reaches out)
intention_signals: Establishes a non-bypassable record of every remote tool call as the accountability spine of the connector, so an unaudited call cannot succeed.
novelty: The explicit write-or-fail-loud audit contract and the conscious choice to disable RLS in favour of JWT-role gating.
contribution: Brings the audit-log substrate and its access model for the remote MCP gateway — the who/scope/tool/outcome record that the gateway and operator UI depend on.
summary_for_embedding: SQL migration creating public.connector_audit, an RLS-off append-only audit table logging every remote MCP gateway tool call (subject/scope/tool/outcome) with indexes and role-based grants.
intention_for_embedding: To make every remote MCP tool call accountable via a mandatory, fail-loud audit log read by the operator UI.
